schools.summary: Paul, continuing the subject of the collection ('the ministering to the saints' — the relief of the poor believers at Jerusalem), says it is 'superfluous (needless)' to write further — they already know their duty and are disposed to it; his tact in commending their willingness while still pressing the matter, a graceful way of encouraging without seeming to doubt them (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Henry): Paul flatters their good will even as he stirs it; the disclaimer that he need not write is itself a spur to their liberality.

themes: as touching the ministering to the saints, it is superfluous for me to write to you
Paul saying it is needless to write further
commending their readiness while pressing the matter
the graceful opening of the appeal

schools.summary: Paul 'knows the forwardness (eagerness, readiness) of your mind' — sure of their willing disposition — and on its strength had 'boasted of you to them of Macedonia, that Achaia (the province of which Corinth was chief) was ready a year ago'; and the Corinthians' 'zeal' had 'provoked (stirred up, stimulated) very many' of the Macedonians to give — zeal is contagious (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): Paul had used the Corinthians' eagerness to stir the Macedonians, and now uses the Macedonians' example to stir the Corinthians — each church provoking the other to good works.

themes: I know the forwardness of your mind, for which I boast of you to them of Macedonia
your zeal hath provoked very many
boasting of the Corinthians' readiness
the mutual stimulation of zeal

schools.summary: Despite his confidence, Paul 'sent the brethren' (Titus and the two companions of ch. 8) 'lest our boasting of you should be in vain (proved empty) in this behalf' — to ensure his boast was not put to shame; the purpose that the Corinthians might be fully 'ready' with their gift when the time came, the brethren sent to see the collection completed beforehand (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul does not presume on their good will but takes practical means to secure the result; confidence and prudent action go together.

themes: yet have I sent the brethren, lest our boasting of you should be in vain
that, as I said, ye may be ready
the brethren sent so the giving would be ready
confidence joined with prudence

schools.summary: Paul's specific fear: that 'if they of Macedonia come with me, and find you unprepared (the collection not ready),' both he and they would be put to shame 'in this same confident boasting'; his tact in saying 'we should be ashamed' — placing the shame first on himself — and only by gentle implication adding 'that we say not, ye,' sparing them the direct charge while making the point (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).
Calvin: the honour of the Corinthians, of Paul, and of the cause were all at stake; Paul's concern was that all be spared the embarrassment of an empty boast.

themes: lest... they of Macedonia... find you unprepared
we (that we say not, ye) should be ashamed in this same confident boasting
avoiding shame if found unprepared
Paul's delicate care

schools.summary: Paul 'thought it necessary to exhort the brethren' to 'go before (ahead of him)' to Corinth and 'make up beforehand your bounty (the generous gift)' — to have the collection gathered and ready before his arrival; the gift to be 'as a matter of bounty (a generous, free offering)' and 'not as of covetousness (not grudgingly, as something extorted)' — given freely, not squeezed out as if reluctantly (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): by gathering it beforehand and at leisure, the gift would be a true freewill offering, not a hurried, reluctant collection.

themes: make up beforehand your bounty... as a matter of bounty, and not as of covetousness
the brethren sent ahead to prepare the gift
a willing bounty, not a grudging sum
the gift readied as a freewill offering

schools.summary: Paul lays down a general law (drawn from agriculture): as in sowing, so in giving — 'he which soweth sparingly (scantily, grudgingly) shall reap also sparingly' (a stingy giver reaps a scanty return), and 'he which soweth bountifully (liberally, with blessings) shall reap also bountifully' (the generous giver reaps an abundant harvest); God rewards liberality liberally (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin (with Wesley): the reaping is both spiritual and (as God sees fit) temporal; giving is a sowing that God repays, so liberality is never loss.

themes: He which soweth sparingly shall reap also sparingly; and he which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully
the law of the harvest applied to giving
liberal sowing, abundant reaping
giving a sowing God rewards

schools.summary: Giving should follow a 'purpose of the heart' — a deliberate, settled resolve (not a passing impulse, a spent emotion, nor a half-regretted promise), each giving from a willing heart, the heart being more concerned in giving than the head; 'not grudgingly (of grief, as if sorry to part with it) nor of necessity (by constraint, as if forced)'; for 'God loveth a cheerful (hilaron, glad, hilarious) giver,' delighting in the willing heart (an echo of the Greek of Proverbs 22:8) — Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson.

Calvin: liberality is estimated by God not from the sum but from the disposition; alms are a sacrifice, and no sacrifice pleases God unless voluntary — he requires not a tyrant's extortion but the cheerful, filial obedience of children; Clarke adds the Jewish distinction between the grudging alms of legal necessity and the cheerful freewill offering God loves.

themes: as he purposeth in his heart, so let him give; not grudgingly, or of necessity
for God loveth a cheerful giver
willing and cheerful giving
alms a voluntary sacrifice God loves

schools.summary: Paul's encouragement to the giver: 'God is able to make all grace abound toward you' — to supply, overflowingly, every needed grace and good thing — so that they need not fear that giving will leave them in want; the comprehensive promise 'always (at all times), all sufficiency (autarkeia, enough, contentment, competence), in all things,' God supplying the giver fully; that, so supplied, they 'may abound to every good work,' their sufficiency overflowing in further generosity (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: God supplies the giver that he may give the more; the promise frees liberality from the fear of want, for God makes all grace abound to those who give.

themes: God is able to make all grace abound toward you
always having all sufficiency in all things, may abound to every good work
God's power to supply all sufficiency
supplied to give the more

schools.summary: Paul quotes Psalm 112:9 (of the righteous, God-fearing man): 'he hath dispersed abroad (scattered freely, as a sower); he hath given to the poor' — a picture of open-handed, generous giving — and 'his righteousness (his beneficence, his right conduct, here especially his charity) remaineth for ever,' not lost but abiding, in its fruit and reward, perpetually (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: 'righteousness' here includes the works of mercy; the generous man's good deeds are not transient but endure before God, who remembers and rewards them.

themes: He hath dispersed abroad; he hath given to the poor: his righteousness remaineth for ever
the scriptural picture of the liberal man
scattering freely to the poor
righteousness that endures

schools.summary: Paul affirms (or prays) that God, 'he that ministereth (supplieth) seed to the sower and bread for food' (Isaiah 55:10), will 'both minister bread for your food' (supply the giver's own needs) 'and multiply your seed sown' (increase his means of giving) and 'increase the fruits of your righteousness' — the harvest of their charity, both in its further effects and its reward; he multiplies both the seed (means) and the fruit (result) of their giving (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).
Calvin: as God supplies the natural sower with seed and bread, so he will supply and multiply the spiritual sower's means and reward; the giver loses nothing but gains increase.

themes: he that ministereth seed to the sower both minister bread for your food, and multiply your seed sown
increase the fruits of your righteousness
God supplying and multiplying the giver's seed and fruit
the giver gains increase

schools.summary: The givers are 'enriched in every thing to all bountifulness (liberality)' — God enriches them not for hoarding but for the purpose of generous giving; and this liberality, 'through us' (through Paul's administration of the gift), 'causeth thanksgiving to God' — the relief of the poor saints leads them to thank God; giving produces gratitude and praise to him (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): the chain — God enriches the giver, the giver relieves the poor, and the poor thank God — so the whole flows back to God's glory.

themes: being enriched in every thing to all bountifulness
which causeth through us thanksgiving to God
enriched for bountifulness that yields thanksgiving
liberality returning to God's glory

schools.summary: 'The administration of this service (the collection, the ministering)' has a twofold result: it 'not only supplieth the want of the saints' (relieves the material need of the poor believers at Jerusalem) but is 'abundant (overflowing) also by many thanksgivings unto God' — it overflows in the gratitude and praise that the relieved saints render to God; the spiritual fruit exceeds the material relief (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the giving is doubly valuable — it meets bodily need and it multiplies praise to God; the highest end of charity is the thanksgiving it brings forth.

themes: the administration of this service not only supplieth the want of the saints
but is abundant also by many thanksgivings unto God
the collection relieving want and abounding in thanksgiving
the double fruit of charity

schools.summary: 'By the experiment (the proof, the tested evidence) of this ministration (the collection),' the relieved saints 'glorify God' — the gift was a demonstration that moved them to praise God for the givers — on two grounds: the Corinthians' 'professed subjection (obedience, confession) unto the gospel of Christ' (their genuine faith, proved by deeds) and their 'liberal distribution (generous sharing) unto them and unto all men' (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): the liberality was visible evidence that the Corinthians' profession of the gospel was real; their giving authenticated their faith and brought glory to God.

themes: by the experiment of this ministration they glorify God for your professed subjection unto the gospel
and for your liberal distribution unto them, and unto all men
the saints glorifying God for obedience and liberality
giving authenticating faith

schools.summary: The further fruit of the gift: the relieved saints respond 'by their prayer for you' (they pray for the Corinthians) and 'long after you' (yearn for you with affection); the gift binds the churches in love; the ground of their love and prayer being 'the exceeding (surpassing) grace of God in you' — the abundant grace seen in the Corinthians' liberality and faith drew out the saints' affection and intercession (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the gift created a holy reciprocity — the Corinthians gave material aid, and the Jerusalem saints returned love and prayer; the communion of saints was strengthened.

themes: by their prayer for you, which long after you
for the exceeding grace of God in you
the saints' prayer and longing love
the communion of saints strengthened by giving

schools.summary: Paul closes the whole discourse on giving with a sudden burst of praise: 'thanks be unto God for his unspeakable (indescribable, beyond words) gift' — the contemplation of grace overflows into thanksgiving; the 'unspeakable gift' is, most fully, Christ himself — God's supreme, indescribable gift (John 3:16) — though some include the whole grace of the gospel and the fruits of liberality just described; the gift that surpasses all words and is the pattern and source of all Christian generosity (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley).

themes: Thanks be unto God for his unspeakable gift
the doxology of thanks
God's unspeakable gift of Christ
the fountain of all generosity
